metadata.title: Why assembly shrinks through 13 sequential filters, not one
metadata.purpose: Peephole passes form a dependency chain — local passes clear simple patterns, a global pass fuses across blocks, cleanup passes handle tail-calls and frame-pointer elision the earlier passes unlocked.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: instruction encodings, register allocation, full pass-name list
metadata.visual_object: A row of assembly boxes, each smaller than the last, annotated with which pass removed which instruction.
metadata.hook: The backend emits raw assembly, then runs 13 peephole passes — each finds patterns the previous pass first had to make visible.

beats.narration_text: The dead pair `mov %eax,%ebx; mov %ebx,%eax` survives until pass 7, because an earlier dead-store elimination first removes a store that made the moves look live.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Why isn't the same peephole pattern caught in a single pass? What does pass N produce that lets pass N+1 see a pattern it couldn't before?
The backend emits raw assembly, then runs 13 peephole passes — each finds patterns the previous pass first had to make visible.

Peephole passes form a dependency chain — local passes clear simple patterns, a global pass fuses across blocks, cleanup passes handle tail-calls and frame-pointer elision the earlier passes unlocked.
beats.visual: A row of assembly boxes, each smaller than the last, annotated with which pass removed which instruction.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: `mov %rax,%rbx; call foo; mov %rbx,%rax` (result unused) → dead-call pass kills side effects → dead-move pass removes both moves.

Peephole passes form a dependency chain — local passes clear simple patterns, a global pass fuses across blocks, cleanup passes handle tail-calls and frame-pointer elision the earlier passes unlocked.
